questions.stem: Aggregate demand (AD) is the total planned spending on domestic goods and services. Which expression represents it?
questions.explanation: Aggregate demand is the sum of consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports: $AD = C + I + G + (X - M)$.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The aggregate demand curve slopes downward, showing that a lower price level is associated with what?
options: A lower quantity of real output demanded
A higher quantity of real output demanded
questions.explanation: As the general price level falls, real wealth rises, interest rates tend to fall, and exports become more competitive, so the quantity of real output demanded increases. Hence AD slopes down.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following would shift the aggregate demand curve to the right?
options: A rise in consumer confidence and spending
A rise in income tax rates
A rise in the savings rate
questions.explanation: Higher consumer confidence raises consumption at every price level, shifting AD right. Higher taxes, lower government spending, and more saving all reduce planned spending and shift AD left.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) measures what?
options: The fraction of each extra unit of income that is spent on consumption
The fraction of income paid in taxes
Total consumption divided by total income
The fraction of income spent on imports only
questions.explanation: MPC is the change in consumption divided by the change in income, that is the share of an extra cedi of income that is consumed rather than saved.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In a simple closed economy with no taxes, if the marginal propensity to consume is 0.8, what is the value of the multiplier?
questions.explanation: The simple multiplier is $1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / (1 - 0.8) = 1 / 0.2 = 5$. An initial injection raises income by five times its size.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The marginal propensity to save (MPS) plus the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) must equal what in a simple two sector model?
questions.explanation: Every extra unit of income is either spent or saved, so $MPC + MPS = 1$. This is why the multiplier can also be written as $1 / MPS$.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An initial increase in investment of 100, in an economy with a multiplier of 4, will eventually change equilibrium national income by how much?
questions.explanation: The change in income equals the multiplier times the initial injection: $4 \times 100 = 400$. The spending circulates round the economy, raising income by more than the original amount.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Why does the multiplier process occur?
options: Taxes rise automatically to fund spending
One person's spending becomes another person's income, which is then partly spent again
The central bank prints money each round
Prices fall continuously after any injection
questions.explanation: An initial injection is received as income by others, who spend a fraction of it (the MPC), creating further income, and so on. The successive rounds sum to a multiple of the original injection.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A larger marginal propensity to withdraw (to save, tax, and import) has what effect on the size of the multiplier?
options: It has no effect on the multiplier
questions.explanation: The full multiplier is $1 / MPW$, where MPW is the marginal propensity to withdraw. A larger MPW means more leaks out each round, so the multiplier is smaller.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the long run, the aggregate supply curve is usually drawn as vertical. What does this imply?
options: Output is determined by the economy's productive capacity, not the price level
Output can rise without limit as prices rise
Higher prices always raise long run output
The price level is fixed in the long run
questions.explanation: A vertical long run AS curve sits at potential output, set by the supply of factors and technology. In the long run, changes in the price level do not change real output, only the price level.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The short run aggregate supply curve typically slopes upward because, as the price level rises, firms find it profitable to do what?
questions.explanation: In the short run some input costs, such as wages, are slow to adjust. When output prices rise, profit margins widen, so firms expand production. This gives an upward sloping short run AS curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Macroeconomic equilibrium in the AD/AS model occurs where what condition holds?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium output and price level are found where the AD and AS curves intersect, that is where total planned spending equals total planned output.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An increase in aggregate demand along an upward sloping short run AS curve will generally do what?
options: Raise both real output and the price level
Lower both real output and the price level
Raise output but lower the price level
questions.explanation: Shifting AD to the right along an upward sloping AS curve moves equilibrium to a higher real output and a higher price level. Both rise together in the short run.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A deflationary (recessionary) gap exists when equilibrium output is where, relative to the full employment level?
options: Exactly at the full employment level
Above the full employment level of output
Below the full employment level of output
questions.explanation: A deflationary gap arises when aggregate demand is too low to achieve full employment, so equilibrium real output lies below potential and unemployment results.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An inflationary gap occurs when aggregate demand is such that equilibrium spending exceeds what?
options: The full employment level of output
questions.explanation: An inflationary gap exists when planned spending at full employment exceeds the economy's capacity to produce. The excess demand pulls up the price level rather than raising real output.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following would shift the short run aggregate supply curve to the left?
options: A sharp rise in oil and raw material prices
questions.explanation: Higher input costs such as oil raise firms' costs of production at every price level, shifting short run AS to the left. Lower taxes, better technology, and lower wages would shift it to the right.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the basic Keynesian model with a horizontal short run AS curve, an increase in aggregate demand below full employment mainly changes what?
options: Real output, with little change in the price level
The price level, with no change in output
questions.explanation: When there is spare capacity and prices are sticky, the AS curve is roughly horizontal, so extra demand is met by producing more at a stable price level. Output rises and inflation stays low.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The consumption function $C = a + bY$ has an intercept $a$ that represents what?
questions.explanation: In $C = a + bY$, the term $a$ is autonomous consumption: the spending that takes place even when income is zero. The slope $b$ is the marginal propensity to consume.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: At the equilibrium level of national income in the Keynesian model, planned saving equals what (in a simple closed economy with no government)?
questions.explanation: In the two sector model, equilibrium requires planned injections to equal planned withdrawals. The only injection is investment and the only withdrawal is saving, so equilibrium is where planned saving equals planned investment.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A fall in the exchange rate (a depreciation), other things equal, tends to shift aggregate demand which way?
options: To the left, as exports fall and imports rise
It does not affect aggregate demand
To the right, as exports rise and imports fall
questions.explanation: A weaker currency makes exports cheaper abroad and imports dearer at home, so net exports rise. Since net exports are a component of AD, the curve shifts to the right.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which component of aggregate demand is usually considered the most volatile?
questions.explanation: Investment depends heavily on expectations, interest rates, and business confidence, so it swings sharply over the cycle. Consumption is comparatively stable because it is tied to current income.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the marginal propensity to consume is 0.75 and the marginal propensity to import is 0.05 (no taxes), the value of the multiplier is approximately:
questions.explanation: Withdrawals are saving and imports. $MPS = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$, plus $MPM = 0.05$, so $MPW = 0.30$. The multiplier is $1 / 0.30 \approx 3.3$.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A rightward shift in long run aggregate supply represents what?
options: A reduction in the labour force
A rise in the price level only
An increase in the economy's productive potential
questions.explanation: Long run AS sits at potential output, so a rightward shift means the economy can produce more at every price level. This reflects growth in resources, technology, or productivity.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A negative supply shock, such as a sudden jump in import prices, tends to cause what combination?
options: Higher prices together with higher output
Lower prices together with higher output
No change in prices or output
Higher prices together with lower output
questions.explanation: A leftward shift of short run AS raises the price level while reducing real output. This combination of rising prices and falling output is often called stagflation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Holding the price level constant, an increase in government spending shifts which curve and in which direction?
questions.explanation: Government spending $G$ is a direct component of aggregate demand. An increase in $G$ raises total planned spending at every price level, shifting the AD curve to the right.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
